question:
Show that every positive rational number can be written as a quotient of products of factorials
of (not necessarily distinct) primes. For example,
\[
\frac{10}{9} = \frac{2!\cdot 5!}{3!\cdot 3! \cdot 3!}.
\]
\,
solution:
Every positive rational number can be uniquely written in lowest terms
as $a/b$ for $a,b$ positive integers. We prove the statement in the
problem by induction on the largest prime dividing either $a$ or $b$
(where this is considered to be $1$ if $a=b=1$). For the base case, we
can write $1/1 = 2!/2!$. For a general $a/b$, let $p$ be the largest
prime dividing either $a$ or $b$; then $a/b = p^k a'/b'$ for some $k\neq
0$ and positive integers $a',b'$ whose largest prime factors are
strictly less than $p$. We now have $a/b = (p!)^k \frac{a'}{(p-1)!^k b'}$,
and all prime factors of $a'$ and $(p-1)!^k b'$ are strictly less than $p$.
By the induction assumption, $\frac{a'}{(p-1)!^k b'}$ can be written as a
quotient of products of prime factorials, and so $a/b = (p!)^k
\frac{a'}{(p-1)!^k b'}$ can as well. This completes the induction.

\textbf{Remark.} Noam Elkies points out that the representations are unique
up to rearranging and canceling common factors.

descriptive_long.question:
Show that every positive rational number can be written as a quotient of products of factorials
of (not necessarily distinct) primes. For example,
\[
\frac{10}{9} = \frac{2!\cdot 5!}{3!\cdot 3! \cdot 3!}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Every positive rational number can be uniquely written in lowest terms
as $numerator/denominator$ for $numerator,denominator$ positive integers. We prove the statement in the
problem by induction on the largest prime dividing either $numerator$ or $denominator$
(where this is considered to be $1$ if $numerator=denominator=1$). For the base case, we
can write $1/1 = 2!/2!$. For a general $numerator/denominator$, let $primefactor$ be the largest
prime dividing either $numerator$ or $denominator$; then $numerator/denominator = primefactor^{exponent} numerator'/denominator'$ for some $exponent\neq
0$ and positive integers $numerator',denominator'$ whose largest prime factors are
strictly less than $primefactor$. We now have $numerator/denominator = (primefactor!)^{exponent} \frac{numerator'}{(primefactor-1)!^{exponent} denominator'}$,
and all prime factors of $numerator'$ and $(primefactor-1)!^{exponent} denominator'$ are strictly less than $primefactor$.
By the induction assumption, $\frac{numerator'}{(primefactor-1)!^{exponent} denominator'}$ can be written as a
quotient of products of prime factorials, and so $numerator/denominator = (primefactor!)^{exponent}
\frac{numerator'}{(primefactor-1)!^{exponent} denominator'}$ can as well. This completes the induction.

\textbf{Remark.} Noam Elkies points out that the representations are unique
up to rearranging and canceling common factors.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Show that every positive rational number can be written as a quotient of products of factorials
of (not necessarily distinct) primes. For example,
\[
\frac{10}{9} = \frac{2!\cdot 5!}{3!\cdot 3! \cdot 3!}.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Every positive rational number can be uniquely written in lowest terms
as $\umbrella/\marigold$ for $\umbrella,\marigold$ positive integers. We prove the statement in the
problem by induction on the largest prime dividing either $\umbrella$ or $\marigold$
(where this is considered to be $1$ if $\umbrella=\marigold=1$). For the base case, we
can write $1/1 = 2!/2!$. For a general $\umbrella/\marigold$, let $\coconuts$ be the largest
prime dividing either $\umbrella$ or $\marigold$; then $\umbrella/\marigold = \coconuts^{\vestibule} \umbrella'/\marigold'$ for some $\vestibule\neq
0$ and positive integers $\umbrella',\marigold'$ whose largest prime factors are
strictly less than $\coconuts$. We now have $\umbrella/\marigold = (\coconuts!)^{\vestibule} \frac{\umbrella'}{(\coconuts-1)!^{\vestibule} \marigold'}$,
and all prime factors of $\umbrella'$ and $(\coconuts-1)!^{\vestibule} \marigold'$ are strictly less than $\coconuts$.
By the induction assumption, $\frac{\umbrella'}{(\coconuts-1)!^{\vestibule} \marigold'}$ can be written as a
quotient of products of prime factorials, and so $\umbrella/\marigold = (\coconuts!)^{\vestibule}
\frac{\umbrella'}{(\coconuts-1)!^{\vestibule} \marigold'}$ can as well. This completes the induction.

\textbf{Remark.} Noam Elkies points out that the representations are unique
up to rearranging and canceling common factors.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Show that every positive rational number can be written as a quotient of products of factorials
of (not necessarily distinct) primes. For example,
\[
\frac{10}{9} = \frac{2!\cdot 5!}{3!\cdot 3! \cdot 3!}.
\]
\,
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Every positive rational number can be uniquely written in lowest terms
as $irrational/infinite$ for $irrational,infinite$ positive integers. We prove the statement in the
problem by induction on the largest prime dividing either $irrational$ or $infinite$
(where this is considered to be $1$ if $irrational=infinite=1$). For the base case, we
can write $1/1 = 2!/2!$. For a general $irrational/infinite$, let $composite$ be the largest
prime dividing either $irrational$ or $infinite$; then $irrational/infinite = composite^{fractional} irrational'/infinite'$ for some $fractional\neq
0$ and positive integers $irrational',infinite'$ whose largest prime factors are
strictly less than $composite$. We now have $irrational/infinite = (composite!)^{fractional} \frac{irrational'}{(composite-1)!^{fractional} infinite'}$,
and all prime factors of $irrational'$ and $(composite-1)!^{fractional} infinite'$ are strictly less than $composite$.
By the induction assumption, $\frac{irrational'}{(composite-1)!^{fractional} infinite'}$ can be written as a
quotient of products of prime factorials, and so $irrational/infinite = (composite!)^{fractional}
\frac{irrational'}{(composite-1)!^{fractional} infinite'}$ can as well. This completes the induction.

\textbf{Remark.} Noam Elkies points out that the representations are unique
up to rearranging and canceling common factors.

garbled_string.question:
Show that every positive rational number can be written as a quotient of products of factorials
of (not necessarily distinct) primes. For example,
\[
\frac{10}{9} = \frac{2!\cdot 5!}{3!\cdot 3! \cdot 3!}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Every positive rational number can be uniquely written in lowest terms
as $qzxwvtnp/hjgrksla$ for $qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla$ positive integers. We prove the statement in the
problem by induction on the largest prime dividing either $qzxwvtnp$ or $hjgrksla$
(where this is considered to be $1$ if $qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla=1$). For the base case, we
can write $1/1 = 2!/2!$. For a general $qzxwvtnp/hjgrksla$, let $mdfqvczx$ be the largest
prime dividing either $qzxwvtnp$ or $hjgrksla$; then $qzxwvtnp/hjgrksla = mdfqvczx^{lksnqwer} qzxwvtnp'/hjgrksla'$ for some $lksnqwer\neq
0$ and positive integers $qzxwvtnp',hjgrksla'$ whose largest prime factors are
strictly less than $mdfqvczx$. We now have $qzxwvtnp/hjgrksla = (mdfqvczx!)^{lksnqwer} \frac{qzxwvtnp'}{(mdfqvczx-1)!^{lksnqwer} hjgrksla'}$,
and all prime factors of $qzxwvtnp'$ and $(mdfqvczx-1)!^{lksnqwer} hjgrksla'$ are strictly less than $mdfqvczx$.
By the induction assumption, $\frac{qzxwvtnp'}{(mdfqvczx-1)!^{lksnqwer} hjgrksla'}$ can be written as a
quotient of products of prime factorials, and so $qzxwvtnp/hjgrksla = (mdfqvczx!)^{lksnqwer}
\frac{qzxwvtnp'}{(mdfqvczx-1)!^{lksnqwer} hjgrksla'}$ can as well. This completes the induction.

\textbf{Remark.} Noam Elkies points out that the representations are unique
up to rearranging and canceling common factors.

kernel_variant.question:
Show that every positive rational number can be expressed as a quotient of products of factorials of (not necessarily distinct) prime numbers. For instance,
\[
\frac{21}{20}=\frac{7!\,\cdot 3!}{5!\,\cdot 5!\,\cdot 2!}.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Write any positive rational number in lowest terms as a/b with positive integers a,b.  We prove by induction on the largest prime dividing either a or b that a/b can be written as a quotient of products of prime factorials.

Base case.  If a=b=1 the largest prime is defined to be 1.  We may write

    1/1 = 3!/3! ,

expressing the unit fraction with prime factorials.

Inductive step.  Suppose the statement holds whenever the largest prime divisor of numerator or denominator is smaller than some fixed prime p.  Consider a fraction a/b whose largest such prime is exactly p.  Then

    a/b = p^k \cdot  (a'/b')

for some nonzero integer k and positive integers a',b' whose prime factors are all strictly less than p.

Isolate the power of p with factorials:

    p^k = (p!)^k / ((p-1)!)^k.

Hence

    a/b = (p!)^k \cdot  ( a' / ( (p-1)!)^k b' ).

All prime factors in the latter fraction are less than p, so by the induction hypothesis it can itself be written as a quotient of products of prime factorials.  Multiplying by the explicit prime factorial (p!)^k we obtain such a representation for a/b as well.  This completes the induction and the proof.

For example, 21/20 = 7! \cdot  3! / (5! \cdot  5! \cdot  2!) as displayed in the statement.

core_steps: Write the rational number a/b in lowest terms
Induct on the largest prime p dividing either numerator or denominator
Isolate the p–power:  a/b = p^k · a'/b' with k ≠ 0 and smaller–prime part a'/b'
Apply the induction hypothesis to a' / ( (p−1)! )^k b' to finish

slot1.description: The illustrative rational number used in the statement as an example

slot2.description: The particular factorial decomposition shown for that example

slot3.description: The factorial chosen to express 1 in the base case of the induction
